0.use: Text-only cover: strong verbal headline + 230-char dek + 2-3 topic tag pills, big left-aligned type; when there is no hero asset. Title-alone slab → #135.
0.avoid: Don't use when a chart or image is the hook — siblings 1-7 carry media; tag pills with one tag look bare.

1.use: Cover where TEXT leads and a hero image supports: eyebrow/headline/dek on top, large landscape image below. Pick #2 instead if the image itself is the hook.
1.avoid: Needs a genuinely good landscape image; a filler screenshot makes the bottom half read as dead space; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

2.use: Cover where the IMAGE is the hook, seen first: large landscape image up top, headline+dek beneath. Sibling of #1 — pick #1 when the headline should lead.
2.avoid: Weak or abstract imagery kills it; the top half is the first thing scrolled past; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

3.use: Cover teasing BOTH an image and a chart: text column left, stacked image+chart rail right, plus a CTA pill. Pick #7 for 3 visuals, #6 for chart-only.
3.avoid: Don't fill both right slots with placeholders just to use the split; two weak visuals beat one strong one never; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

4.use: Full-bleed atmospheric image covers the whole slide, headline card overlaid at bottom. Pick when one cinematic photo carries the mood and text can sit on it.
4.avoid: Requires a full-frame image with quiet lower third; busy images fight the overlaid text card.

5.use: Stat-led cover: one giant number (16 chars max) IS the headline, dek explains it, small chart backs it; pick only when a single figure is the hook.
5.avoid: Don't force a soft metric into the hero slot; if the number needs a sentence to matter, use #6 instead.

6.use: Chart-led cover: headline + short dek (99 chars) over a chart filling the lower two-thirds. Pick over #5 when the trend shape, not one number, is the hook.
6.avoid: The dek is tight at 99 chars; if the chart needs real explanation this is the wrong opener.

7.use: Collage cover for THREE distinct visuals (2 images + 1 chart) teasing a multi-part visual tour; headline+dek on top. Pick #3 when you only have two assets.
7.avoid: Empty or repetitive tiles read instantly as filler — only use with 3 genuinely different assets; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

8.use: Text-only cover with a gradient brand rail + series footer label; recurring-series/newsletter opener. Pick #0 for topic tags, #9 for centered CTA.
8.avoid: Mostly empty middle — headline and dek must carry it; skip for one-off posts with no series identity.

9.use: Centered poster-style text cover with a CTA pill: one thesis sentence as headline, symmetric layout. Pick for opinionated bottom-line openers with no media.
9.avoid: Headlines past ~2 lines break the centered symmetry; not for data-led posts (use #5/#6).

17.use: Cover when two real images (photos/renders/charts) set up an A-vs-B or before/after story; text is eyebrow + short headline + one-line subhead.
17.avoid: Needs both images supplied — empty portrait frames read as placeholders; long headlines crowd the right edge; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

21.use: Cover that previews a sequenced story: headline plus a bottom card of 3 dated milestones (Q3/Q4/H1-style label + a 3-5 word note each).
21.avoid: Milestone notes cap at ~25 chars; skip when the story has no real dates or ordered phases.

24.use: Flagship opener: centered magazine masthead with issue/date line + footer tag; pure-text brand ceremony. Giant left slab → #135; metadata imprint → #157.
24.avoid: Too ceremonial for routine posts — slide 1 of a flagship deck only, never mid-deck.

25.use: Agenda/TOC slide: numbered 01-05 rows naming the 4-5 sections the deck actually covers, one short title per row.
25.avoid: Rows are section titles, not takeaways; under 4 real sections it reads padded (row 5 caps at 28 chars).

29.use: Four-row entity-to-verdict table: names left, one-word status or number right (TSMC → Lead); for a watchlist with a call per row. Letter grades → #94.
29.avoid: Right-hand cells fit ~16 chars — a word or number, never prose; padding to 4 rows reads as filler.

31.use: Sequential three-step explainer: numbered 01-03 cards, each a short step name plus a one-line reason — how-it-works or why-X-happens chains.
31.avoid: Exactly 3 steps with one-line bodies; don't force unordered lists into numbered steps.

33.use: The 'so what' slide: a 1-2 sentence takeaway body, one implication statement in a highlight pill, and 3 keyword tag chips underneath.
33.avoid: Needs a genuine one-line implication plus 3 real tags; without them the pill card leaves the lower third empty.

34.use: Heat-map slide: chart slot renders a cell grid of where risk/strength concentrates; text is title + 1-line caption. Sibling of #38 — 2D grids here, flows there.
34.avoid: Needs a real matrix chart in the slot; without data the gradient cells are pure decoration.

36.use: Two labeled position cards (bull/bear) each carrying one full ~120-char sentence — prose face-off with no scores. To score and call a winner → #58/#149.
36.avoid: Two contenders only — 3+ options need a table; one sentence per card, no lists inside.

38.use: Value-chain flow: sankey slot for quantities moving between stages (wafers→systems→revenue); title + caption only. Sibling of #34 — flows here, grids there.
38.avoid: Requires a real flow dataset in the chart slot; the ribbon blocking is decorative without one.

39.use: A containment hierarchy of up to 6 named layers (model→chip→rack→grid); indented rows read as nesting, so pick only when levels genuinely sit inside each other.
39.avoid: Not for flat lists or rankings — the alternating indent implies parent/child; for % shares use #55.

40.use: Verbatim pull-quote from the source article: giant quote mark, 72-char line + 145-char context. Named speaker w/ headshot → #96; own thesis as quote → #139.
40.avoid: Don't paraphrase into it — if the line isn't quote-worthy on its own, use #41's verdict card instead.

41.use: Your own called conclusion after evidence slides: 'Verdict' badge, one-line ruling, 150-char justification; mid-deck or closer. Verbatim source quote → #40.
41.avoid: Needs an actual ruling; a neutral summary wastes the Verdict badge.

43.use: One dominant number (multiplier/percent, ≤12 chars) that IS the story, with a 6-bar mini trend beneath for context; hero stat leads, chart supports.
43.avoid: Needs both the hero figure and 6 trend points; if the chart itself is the story, use #52.

44.use: A single strong portrait-crop image that speaks for itself; text is a margin note only (91-char body in a narrow side rail, no headline slot).
44.avoid: No headline — don't use when the slide needs framing text; headline-led single image is #48; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

46.use: Four related images shown as a set — recap gallery, four examples or variants — in a 2x2 thumb grid under one headline.
46.avoid: Needs all 4 real images; empty or filler tiles read as unfinished; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

48.use: One diagram or screenshot carries the slide, framed by a headline above and a one-line caption below; big square image slot. Portrait image, no headline → #44.
48.avoid: Image slot, not chart — for a data chart use #52; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

52.use: A single chart (waterfall/bar) is the whole story: headline frames it, 63-char caption closes it; accepts a chart module, not an image. Hero-number-led → #43.
52.avoid: Don't fill the chart slot with a decorative graphic; the slide has nothing else to fall back on.

55.use: Composition breakdown: 3-4 parts of a whole, each a % share drawn as a filled bar (power budget, cost split); shares should sum to ~100. Nesting → #39.
55.avoid: Bar length encodes magnitude — unrelated stats or non-share numbers make the bars lie.

58.use: Compact A-vs-B scoreboard: TWO contenders, 3 shared metric rows, VS medallion, edge-call pill. Full spec cards + WINNER badge → #149; 3+ options need a table.
58.avoid: Requires a callable edge — skip if you can't name a winner, and never squeeze in a third contender.

59.use: Exactly three headline numbers that tell one story — each value <=12 chars ('+38%', '4.6x') with an <=18-char label; staggered big-type stat strip.
59.avoid: Value slots are 12 chars — numbers only, no phrases; don't pad to three with a stat that isn't load-bearing.

60.use: Like #59 but for THREE 0-100 readings (utilization %, share, score) rendered as ring gauges; pick #59 when values aren't percent-like.
60.avoid: Rings imply proportion of a whole — arbitrary magnitudes ($, MW, multiples) read wrong in a gauge.

61.use: A trend chart whose story hinges on ONE moment — a pinned callout (16-char tag + 120-char sentence) explains it; the chart slot supplies the plot.
61.avoid: Needs a real chart in the slot and a single genuine key moment; multi-point stories overload the one callout.

71.use: Prose slide: 2-3 sentence argument paragraph (<=336 chars) in a card with 4 topic chips + 190-char framing subhead. Longer sourced analyst note → #182.
71.avoid: Subhead and body render as adjacent paragraphs — give them different jobs or the slide says the same thing twice.

76.use: Numbered chapter divider for sequential takeaways — headline names the point, 150-char subhead states it; eyebrow carries the step number.
76.avoid: Ghost numeral is hardcoded '01' (no field) — on steps 2+ the giant background digit won't match your eyebrow.

86.use: Deck opener: one provocative hook sentence (<=72 chars) in giant type with a SWIPE cue; module slot can fill the empty lower third.
86.avoid: Slide-1 only, one per deck; without a module the bottom third is dead space.

90.use: Market-tape moment: 4 metric->value console rows ('hbm.alloc: tight', 'signal: RISK-ON'); values may be status words, unlike #59's big-number strip.
90.avoid: Terminal chrome is a strong flavor — off-tone for non-market topics; a large empty band sits between headline and card.

94.use: Graded ranking of up to 5 vendors where each row earns a letter grade in a color-coded chip (ClusterMAX-style); needs a real grading scheme.
94.avoid: Don't reach for it without differentiated grades — five A's or invented letters read as filler.

96.use: A quote a named person actually SAID, on the record: headshot slot, 17-char name, 31-char title. Article pull-quote → #40; own thesis → #139.
96.avoid: The quote IS the headline field — no paragraph capacity; short quotes leave the bottom third empty.

98.use: Market-sizing narrative: 4 shrinking tiers (TAM->serviceable->demand->won), each a name plus a hard dollar figure; bar widths encode the narrowing.
98.avoid: Values must genuinely shrink top-to-bottom; bottom tier chip is low-contrast brown, so keep its label short.

101.use: Ranked leaderboard of exactly 4 contenders, short name + numeric score per row, #1 row visually crowned; needs a real ranking with a clear winner.
101.avoid: No true ranking = filler rows; for 5 entries with value-scaled chart bars pick #117.

104.use: Hero forecast number + delta chip, 1-2 sentence why, trend chart, three timeline milestone stats — metric-with-trajectory. Bare number + mini trend → #43.
104.avoid: Needs a real time series behind the number; without chart data the mid-band goes dead.

107.use: Share-of-market split across 2-3 stack layers (e.g. compute/HBM/packaging), each bar segmented by named players with proportional widths.
107.avoid: Needs actual share splits per layer; single-metric rankings belong in #101 or #117.

109.use: Subscribe-conversion closer: benefit headline, one-line value prop, three feature chips, subscribe-URL button. Ask is signup — follows go to #111.
109.avoid: One closer per deck, last slide; skip if you can't name 3 real member benefits for the chips.

111.use: Follow-us closer with three platform-to-handle rows (X / LinkedIn / newsletter). Pick when the ask is a follow; subscribe button = #109, no-ask = #112.
111.avoid: Last or second-to-last slide only; rows need real handles, never invented placeholders.

112.use: No-ask signoff led by the big WORDMARK: thanks kicker + tagline + URL; ends the deck quiet instead of a CTA (#109/#111/#116). Seal + final call line → #130.
112.avoid: Pure brand moment, at most one per deck; adding stats or extra copy breaks the restraint.

113.use: Next-installment teaser for a real multi-part series: coming-topic headline, drop-timing line, and a PART X OF Y progress pill.
113.avoid: Only when a sequel genuinely exists; the part pill must match the true series position.

116.use: Engagement closer built on ONE direct question to readers plus a comments-CTA pill; goal is replies, not subscribes (#109) or follows (#111).
116.avoid: Headline must read as a question — the quote-mark ornament turns statements into fake quote cards.

117.use: Five-way magnitude race: label + one comparable number per row, bars scaled to value with the figure inside. Pick over #101 for 5 entries and chart feel.
117.avoid: All values must share one unit or normalized index; mixed units make bar widths lie.

123.use: Exactly FOUR headline metrics in a 2x2 grid, big number + micro label each — quarterly scorecard. Three stats → #59, six → #159, one hero → #160.
123.avoid: Caption-length labels only, no room for why; padding to fill all 4 cells reads as filler.

124.use: One widely believed claim you can flatly debunk: myth vs reality one-liners side by side, with a 230-char subhead to frame why the distinction matters.
124.avoid: Needs a genuinely wrong belief; a soft 'true, but' nuance makes the X-vs-check framing overwrought.

130.use: Closer led by the brand SEAL icon plus one last call line ('That is the call.') + 150-char sign-off; wordmark-led quiet end → #112. Last slide only.
130.avoid: Not a content slide — no evidence capacity; anywhere before the final slide it reads as a premature goodbye.

131.use: Momentum break teasing the next section of a long deck ('Next: ...') with dots + chevron; needs art in its module slot — the four panels ship empty.
131.avoid: Renders as a skeleton — 4 ghost panels, one slot, body text clips bottom-right; never use as a content slide.

132.use: 'Save this' cheat sheet: 4 label→value rows of hard reference numbers (specs, dates) under a bookmark CTA; earn it with facts readers will want later.
132.avoid: Values max 24 chars — facts only, no sentences; fewer than 4 real reference numbers makes the bookmark ask hollow.

135.use: Type-only cover: declarative <=72-char title at giant left-aligned slab scale, issue/date footers, no art. Centered masthead → #24; short centered title → #188.
135.avoid: The headline stands alone across ~5 display lines; hedged or wordy titles have nothing to hide behind.

137.use: Investigative 'case file' cover — FILE/REF header, CONFIDENTIAL stamp, Subject/Date/Status panel; earned by who-controls/inside-X stories with real metadata.
137.avoid: Costume-y on generic topics; as-rendered the stamp covers the CLEARANCE value — check that corner.

139.use: Your own thesis (<=72 chars) styled as a giant quote between quote marks, attribution footer. Verbatim source line → #40; highlighted-phrase TL;DR → #172.
139.avoid: Not for paraphrase or multi-sentence points; a bland line at display scale just amplifies the blandness.

141.use: Rank reshuffle between exactly two periods across 5 named players (who passed whom), with share % on the right; the line crossings are the story.
141.avoid: Line art is baked to the demo ranking — text edits won't re-route crossings; a flat no-change ranking wastes the device.

142.use: Then-vs-now shift per metric: 5 rows, two dots on a shared track with values at each end. For magnitude deltas; pick #141 when rank order is the story.
142.avoid: Row 5 collides with the footer at the canvas edge as-rendered; prefer 4 rows or verify the bottom.

143.use: Actual-vs-target per item: 4 bullet bars, big % + red plan tick each — yield/utilization against goal; #142 is for two-time-point deltas instead.
143.avoid: Fourth card and TARGET legend sit clipped at the canvas edge as-rendered; verify or run 3 rows.

144.use: Six categories each scored on two opposing measures (supply vs demand, ours vs theirs) — mirrored tornado bars with a number at each end of every row.
144.avoid: Bar widths are fixed geometry, not data-bound — don't use when readers will check bar length against the numbers.

148.use: One decision with 4 real upsides AND 4 real downsides, each a full clause — balanced pros/cons verdict on a single named subject (pill at top).
148.avoid: Needs both columns genuinely full; a lopsided case (1-2 cons) leaves a limp half.

149.use: TWO full-height contender cards, 3 numeric spec rows each, declared WINNER badge — GPU-vs-ASIC matchup. Compact edge-pill scoreboard → #58; never 3+ options.
149.avoid: Skip if you won't call a winner — the badge forces a verdict; card interiors look empty without a module fill.

153.use: Generation-over-generation spec table where exactly ONE row moved — the '1 spec moved' pill spotlights a single delta among otherwise-identical rows.
153.avoid: If several specs changed, the spot-the-change framing collapses; use #149 for a real matchup.

157.use: Report imprint opener/closer: big title + six metadata pairs (series, issue, author, coverage, date, source); at most one per deck. Ceremonial masthead → #24.
157.avoid: No argument capacity — it's an imprint; bottom third sits empty unless the module slot is filled.

159.use: Six co-equal headline numbers about one subject — 3x2 'by the numbers' stat wall, label + big value per tile; pick #160 when ONE number is the story.
159.avoid: Don't pad to six with weak stats — filler tiles are obvious; no room for per-stat context.

160.use: One record/milestone number as the gradient hero banner, backed by 4 context rows (prior record, margin, timing); pick #159 for six equal stats.
160.avoid: RECORD framing over-promises for routine figures — reserve for genuine firsts or superlatives.

162.use: Four parallel workstreams laid on a Q1-Q4 axis with a NOW marker — when relative timing and overlap of ramps/quals IS the argument.
162.avoid: Bar spans are fixed decoration, not data-bound — only labels edit; don't imply precise start/end dates.

163.use: Sequential A-D gate chain with a PASS/BLOCKED/PENDING pill per stage — for one-bottleneck-stalls-the-pipeline stories; pick #165 for progress up a stack.
163.avoid: Needs a genuine choke point; an all-PASS chain has no tension and reads as a filler checklist.

165.use: Five-layer build sequence read bottom-up (01→05) with a status chip per step — 'how far along is the buildout' stories; pick #163 when one BLOCKED gate is it.
165.avoid: Bottom-to-top reading order suits infra stacks; a plain chronological how-to will fight the layout.

166.use: Eight named parts each with a % share, drawn as a fixed die-floorplan treemap — the share-of-whole slide for area/cost/capacity budgets.
166.avoid: Block sizes are baked — percentages that don't match the drawn areas mislead; needs all 8 blocks filled.

167.use: Yield/defect story with exactly three hero metrics: wafer dot-map fills the left, each right tile takes a label + big amber number + short unit line.
167.avoid: Wafer graphic is fixed decoration — wrong for non-fab topics or stat counts other than 3.

169.use: One metric across six tiers spanning orders of magnitude, drawn as a descending log-scale bar ladder; each rung takes a name + hard number + unit.
169.avoid: Needs a real magnitude spread across all 6 rungs; the shortest bar crops long unit text.

172.use: One-sentence TL;DR (<=72 chars) set giant with gradient-highlighted key phrases — no quote marks or speaker. Quote framing w/ attribution → #139.
172.avoid: No room for evidence on-slide; if the payoff is a number, reach for a stat/fact card instead.

173.use: A single did-you-know fact whose hero is one number rendered giant inline in the sentence, plus a small 'vs' comparison chip below.
173.avoid: A sentence near the char cap overflows onto the label row and the lower third sits empty — keep the fact very short; leans on its image slot — never pick when no article images are provided

176.use: Steelman rebuttal: the strongest objection stated as a full paragraph, answered by your counter-paragraph in an amber-framed panel below it.
176.avoid: Both panels need real substance; a one-line objection leaves the gray pushback panel hollow.

180.use: Closing 'three things to remember': exactly 3 numbered takeaways, each a bold claim heading plus one caps support sentence.
180.avoid: Fixed at three items — don't pad a weak third takeaway; every item needs its own support sentence.

182.use: Mid-deck analyst-note paragraph (up to ~550 chars) developing one argument, with highlight accents + source attribution; #183 when the prose opens the deck.
182.avoid: Needs a genuinely long paragraph; a short body leaves the note panel hollow.

183.use: Deck-opening standfirst: drop-cap thesis paragraph (~390 chars) + byline + min-read badge — the editorial slide 1 framing; pick #182 for mid-deck prose.
183.avoid: At most one per deck, as the opener; the drop cap styles the first letter, so open the paragraph on a strong word.

184.use: Blueprint cover for layered-hardware topics: fixed 4-layer cross-section diagram with editable callouts + big headline; strong opener or section break.
184.avoid: Diagram always draws the same 4-layer package; callout text can't change the drawing — skip for non-stack topics.

185.use: Cover for chip-internals stories (die, HBM, packaging): fixed die-shot art + 3 legend labels naming die regions. No subhead — pick #186/#190 if one is needed.
185.avoid: Off-silicon topics make the die graphic and anatomy legend a lie; the 3 legend labels are mandatory, not decoration.

186.use: Cover with thermal-contour art for heat/power/density/field-map stories; eyebrow + headline + one ~140-char thesis subhead. Sibling of #190 (circuit metaphor).
186.avoid: Art reads as heatmap/topography — wrong metaphor for market or supply-chain stories.

188.use: Centered type cover or mid-deck divider: kicker + short title + byline on a plain glow gradient. Left-aligned giant slab w/ issue footers → #135.
188.avoid: No subhead or stat capacity; long titles wrap ugly — keep it ~4 punchy words.

190.use: Cover with PCB-trace art for interconnect/packaging/networking/signal stories; ~150-char subhead carries the thesis. Like #186 but circuit, not thermal.
190.avoid: Traces are decorative and fixed — don't pick it hoping to diagram a real topology.

192.use: Scatter/bubble chart card: 5-6 named contenders on two quantitative axes with size as a third measure, plus a one-line takeaway. Needs real positioning data.
192.avoid: Bubble positions/sizes are baked art — labels must plausibly match the drawn layout (one big top-right leader).

193.use: Single share-of-whole percentage told as a 10x10 waffle plus giant stat (utilization, penetration, yield); two legend lines split used vs remaining.
193.avoid: Cell fill is baked near 78/100 — a stat far from ~78% contradicts the grid; not for multi-series data.

194.use: Two-level market map (marimekko): ~3 buyer/segment columns sized by share, each stacked with 2-3 mix percentages. Earn it with a full 11-number dataset.
194.avoid: Rectangle sizes are baked — numbers must roughly match drawn areas; for one-level splits use #197 or #193.

195.use: Six parallel metrics each with a signed delta and unit, one designated breakout — dashboard of 2x3 sparkline cards; middle-right card is pre-highlighted amber.
195.avoid: Sparkline shapes are baked; delta signs must match drawn slopes, and padding to reach six metrics reads as filler.

197.use: One-level cost/capex/BOM breakdown into ~7 parts with one dominant share (~46%) — treemap tiles each take name + %. For 2-level composition use #194.
197.avoid: Tile areas are baked and never reflow — percentages must roughly match drawn sizes and sum to 100.

199.use: Composition shifting across ~5 time periods: 4 components whose shares change, one swelling to dominate — streamgraph + name/% legend pairs. Time-series only.
199.avoid: Band shapes are baked (top band grows); static or shrinking-leader stories contradict the art.
